policy_statement: Develop energy communities: set up at least one energy community and one collective self-consumption project in each neighbourhood.
mechanism_description: Developing energy communities and collective self-consumption projects increases locally shared renewable electricity use, reducing reliance on fossil-based grid generation.
primary_causal_pathway: Renewable electricity deployment + local self-consumption → fossil grid generation displacement → ↓ GHG emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail: (1) Changes the electricity supply/consumption system by organizing citizens/businesses into energy communities that finance/install shared renewables (often PV) and enable collective self-consumption/sharing within a neighbourhood. (2) The mediating process is increased renewable generation and higher local utilization of that generation, which reduces net imports from the grid and lowers the need for fossil/marginal generation (and can reduce curtailment). (3) The climate-relevant outcome is reduced CO₂e emissions from electricity generation (with potential additional reductions if it enables electrification elsewhere, but the core mechanism is generation displacement).
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reducing power-sector GHG emissions by displacing fossil-based electricity generation with locally produced/shared renewable electricity.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: increased locally shared renewable electricity replaces fossil-derived grid electricity, yielding direct GHG reductions.  
2) Why Mitigation over alternatives: while resource efficiency (reduced energy input) is not the main lever and adaptation is not the primary intent/outcome, the central effect is decarbonizing electricity supply. Nature-based solutions are not involved.  
3) Edge cases applied: if a program were purely about social organization without adding renewables or changing grid mix, the mitigation impact could be indirect/uncertain; however, the stated mechanism explicitly increases renewable self-consumption to reduce fossil generation reliance.  
4) Applicability to all policies sharing this mechanism: any policy that develops energy communities/collective self-consumption to increase renewable electricity use inherently targets fossil generation displacement and thus is primarily mitigation.  
5) Note on secondary subtypes: any additional categorization (e.g., equity/affordability, resilience via islanding/storage, demand flexibility) requires policy-specific evidence and is deferred.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: If the “community” effort does not result in additional renewable generation (or if the grid is already near-zero carbon), emissions displacement may be small; conversely, inclusion of storage/microgrids can add adaptation/resilience benefits, but these remain secondary to the dominant mitigation pathway for this mechanism.
key_indicators: “Develop energy communities”; “At least one energy community and one collective self-consumption project”; “set up…in each of the city’s neighbourhoods”
co_benefits: Potential energy affordability/energy poverty reduction; community empowerment and local economic development; grid resilience—mentioned implicitly by the concept but not stated.
instance_edge_case_notes: Could be implemented via incentive programs or municipal facilitation/investment, but the provided text only specifies an outcome target (projects set up per neighbourhood) without naming the delivery instrument.
